context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. - Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m kinda stuck on a 700–800 word reflection abt pro vs anti-development arguments. idk how to frame whether anti-development is utopian, nihilist, or maybe neither.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to flag — that tension is really central to the prompt. Before we go further, let me ask you this: in your own words, what do you think 'anti-development' actually means? What are these thinkers arguing against?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of their thinking process. Rather than scaffolding the concepts for them, I should ask a probing Socratic question that gets them to define their own understanding of the key terms first. This will help them build their own framework before attempting to answer the prompt. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them lead the reasoning.

exchanges.student: i think it’s not literally against helping people, but against the whole idea of “development” as this top-down Western model. maybe they’re arguing it can impose outside values and do harm even when it says it’s helping?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good instinct — you're picking up on the post-development critique fairly well. Now, with that definition in mind, go back to your original question: does that kind of argument sound utopian, nihilist, or neither to you? What's your gut feeling, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown a solid intuitive grasp of the anti-development critique. I should affirm this briefly and then push them to connect it to the utopian/nihilist question, which is where they said they were stuck. This moves the conversation in the direction of the solution without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: my gut says neither, tbh. it feels more like a critique of who gets to define progress, not just “nothing should improve” or “some perfect world will happen somehow.”
exchanges.tutor: Interesting — so if it's a critique of *who defines progress*, what does that imply for planning itself? Can someone hold that critique and still engage in planning, or does it make planning impossible?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and landing on a nuanced position. I should affirm the direction briefly and then push them to think harder about what that position implies for planning - which is the next key tension in the prompt: can one be anti-development and still plan? This moves toward the solution without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: i think they still can plan, just not in that old technocratic way. like maybe planning becomes more participatory/local, but idk if that still counts as “development” in the usual sense.
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something real there. Now here's a question the prompt specifically asks about: *why did these anti-development arguments emerge when they did?* What was happening historically that might have made people start questioning the whole development project?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a strong conceptual leap connecting anti-development critique to alternative planning practices. I should briefly affirm this and then push them toward the historical context question in the prompt, which they haven't touched yet. This is an important part of the assignment and will help them build a more complete reflection.

exchanges.student: maybe after a bunch of postcolonial development projects didn’t deliver what they promised? like states, donors, and experts kept pushing modernization, but ppl saw inequality, dependency, displacement, and failed big plans instead.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're building a historical argument there. So if disillusionment with modernization explains the *rise* of anti-development thinking, what do you think explains its *fall* at some point, and then its comeback more recently?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is connecting the historical emergence of anti-development arguments to concrete failures of modernization projects. This is good progress. I should affirm briefly and push them toward the next part of the prompt they haven't addressed: the rise, fall, and latest rise of anti-development arguments. This will round out their historical analysis.

exchanges.student: i’m a little stuck here ngl. maybe it faded when globalization/neoliberalism got treated like the only realistic path, and now it’s back bc ppl see climate crisis, inequality, and aid/development failures again?
exchanges.tutor: That's a plausible arc — worth developing further in your paper. Now, the prompt also asks whether the pro vs. anti division is too 'Manichean.' Given everything you've said so far, do you think that binary actually holds up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is working through the rise-fall-rise arc reasonably well, even if tentatively. I should affirm the direction and then push them toward the last major piece of the prompt they haven't addressed: the Manichean division question. This will help them synthesize everything into a more nuanced argument for their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: yeah, i don’t think it fully holds up. fr it seems too neat, bc ppl can reject dominant development models but still want justice, infrastructure, health, or better institutions.
exchanges.tutor: You've built up a solid set of ideas across this conversation — the critique of who defines progress, the link to participatory planning, the historical arc, and the limits of the pro/anti binary. It's time to start putting this on paper. What would your opening argument be in the reflection piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through most of the key conceptual tensions in the prompt and has developed a consistent thread of reasoning. We've had 6 exchanges and they have good raw material. This is a good moment to ask them to start integrating their thinking into a written attempt, rather than continuing to explore concepts in chat form. Per the rules on redundancy and integration, I should prompt them to move toward writing.

exchanges.student: maybe my opener is: anti-development isn’t simply utopian or nihilist; it’s a historical critique of dominant development models, esp top-down and Western ones. i want help making that sharper so it doesn’t sound too vague.
exchanges.tutor: The writing has to be entirely yours — I can't help polish or sharpen the language for you. But ask yourself: what specific claim are you actually making? What does your opener *commit* you to arguing for the next 700-800 words? That might help you figure out what feels vague to you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help sharpen their writing, which would cross into editing/polishing territory that I must refuse per the rules. I should firmly but kindly redirect them, reminding them that the writing must be entirely theirs. However, I can ask a Socratic question that helps them think about what specificity their opener is missing, without suggesting language.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, i think i’m claiming anti-development is mainly a critique of power/legitimacy in planning, not a rejection of change itself. does that sound focused enough, or am i still too broad?
exchanges.tutor: I won't tell you if it's focused enough — that's for you to judge. But here's a useful test: does every argument you've developed in our conversation today actually *support* that specific claim? Go through them and see if they all connect.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their thesis, which risks me doing the intellectual work for them. I should not grade or validate the thesis directly, but I can ask them a question that helps them self-evaluate its strength — specifically whether their claim connects to the evidence and arguments they've already built up. I should also remind them I won't be grading their work.

exchanges.student: i think mostly yes, but the rise/fall/comeback part feels less directly tied rn. maybe i need to show how changing historical contexts changed critiques of power, not just list time periods.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp self-correction — connecting historical shifts to evolving critiques of power is much stronger than a timeline. You have a clear thesis, solid arguments, and you've identified your weak spot. You're ready to draft. Go write it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done excellent self-diagnosis here - they've identified exactly where their argument has a gap and proposed a way to fix it on their own. This is a strong moment of independent thinking. I should affirm this briefly and encourage them to move forward with drafting, since we're near the end of our planned exchanges and they have enough to write.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten turns, consistently asking questions that prompted the student to generate their own ideas rather than supplying answers. No submission-ready content was provided, no student text was rewritten, and key ideas were always student-led. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student response before advancing. The tutor gave clear process-level feedback (e.g., Turn 9-10 on thesis focus and the rise/fall arc). Dialogue was progressive with no redundancy, stayed on-task, and responses were appropriately concise. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. The tutor never gave a direct answer, never rewrote student language, and never stated the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it — all key 1.1 triggers are absent. Scaffolding built incrementally on student responses (1.2 fully satisfied). Meta-level feedback was specific and process-oriented, particularly in Turns 8-10 where the tutor redirected the student to self-evaluate thesis focus and argument coherence (1.3 satisfied). Dialogue advanced without repetition and remained anchored to the assignment throughout (2.1 and 2.2 satisfied). Responses were concise, well-phrased, and consistently supportive without implying formal grading (3.1 and 3.2 satisfied).
